questions.q: Identify the best definition of a prototype as described in this episode.
options: An early, incomplete model of a solution built to explore and validate an idea before committing to full development
A complete, production-ready version of a feature released to a small group of users for testing
A formal written specification that lists every requirement before any design work begins
A polished marketing demonstration used to attract investment in a software product
questions.explanation: The script defines a prototype as 'an early, incomplete model of a solution built to explore and validate an idea before committing to full development', and stresses that the key word is doing this cheaply. The tempting distractor is the 'complete, production-ready version released to a small group' — that describes a beta or production release, the opposite of a throwaway prototype, since the whole point is to learn cheaply BEFORE the expensive build.

questions.q: What does the S-W-M-P ("swamp") mnemonic stand for, in order from roughest to most polished?
options: Sketch, Webpage, Model, Product
Storyboard, Wireframe, Mockup, Page
Sketch, Wireframe, Mockup, Prototype
Specification, Wireframe, Mockup, Prototype
questions.explanation: S-W-M-P is the fidelity ladder: Sketch, Wireframe, Mockup, Prototype — said 'swamp' because you climb out of the swamp of vague ideas into a real design. The trap is mixing up the rungs; remember a Mockup is high-fidelity but static, while the Prototype is the interactive, clickable rung that simulates real flows.

questions.q: Why does the episode describe prototyping as "shift-left applied to the user experience"?
options: Because prototypes are read from left to right, like a storyboard of the user's journey
Because it moves design work to the left side of the development team's workflow board
Because it shifts responsibility for usability from developers onto the users being tested
Because it catches problems early, when they are cheap to fix, rather than after an expensive build
questions.explanation: Shift-left means catching problems early when they are cheap to fix — the same logic as modelling before coding (23-02) and code review before integration (25-01). A prototype lets you discover that a layout confuses users on a five-dollar sketch rather than on a five-week build. The other options misread 'left' as a literal direction rather than as moving quality checks earlier in the process.

questions.q: Distinguish a wireframe from a mockup in terms of what each one tests.
options: A wireframe is interactive and tests user flow, while a mockup is static and tests load speed
A wireframe is a high-fidelity coloured design that tests branding, while a mockup tests raw structure
A wireframe is a low-fidelity layout (boxes, lines, no styling) that tests structure and flow, while a mockup is a high-fidelity static design that tests the visual look and brand
A wireframe and a mockup are the same thing, only built at different stages of the project
questions.explanation: A wireframe is low-fidelity — boxes, lines, and placeholder text with deliberately no colours or styling — so it tests structure and user flow. A mockup is high-fidelity but static, showing real colours, fonts and branding, so it tests the visual design. In short: a wireframe tests structure, a mockup tests look. Neither is interactive — that is the Prototype rung — which rules out the option calling a wireframe interactive.

questions.q: According to the episode, what is the most common student error (the "trap") when building a prototype?
options: Polishing a throwaway prototype like production — making it pixel-perfect when its job was only to test the layout
Skipping the sketch stage and starting directly at the wireframe
Showing the prototype to too many users before it is finished
Using placeholder text instead of real content in the wireframe
questions.explanation: The trap is polishing a prototype like production: spending days making a throwaway mockup pixel-perfect when its job was just to test the layout. A prototype is meant to be cheap and disposable, so over-polishing wastes the exact time prototyping was supposed to save. Using placeholder text (option D) is actually correct wireframe practice, not an error.

questions.q: Which recognised framework does the episode use for usability, and which heuristic reduces clutter so attention falls on what matters?
options: WCAG's POUR principles, where 'Robust' removes visual clutter
Nielsen's usability heuristics, where 'aesthetic, minimalist design' removes clutter
The fidelity ladder S-W-M-P, where 'Mockup' minimises styling
The 3-2-1 backup rule, where redundancy reduces interface clutter
questions.explanation: Usability is framed around Nielsen's ten usability heuristics, and 'aesthetic, minimalist design' is the one that removes clutter to reduce cognitive load — the same minimalism as the slide-design rule from presenting (25-02). POUR (option A) is the accessibility framework, not the usability one, so confusing the two is the tempting error.

questions.q: What do the four POUR accessibility principles stand for?
options: Portable, Open, Usable, Reliable
Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust
Perceivable, Operable, Usable, Responsive
Predictable, Operable, Understandable, Resilient
questions.explanation: POUR — Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust — organises the W-C-A-G (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines). Perceivable means alt text, contrast and captions; Operable means keyboard access and focus indicators; Understandable means clear language and helpful errors; Robust means semantic markup that works with assistive technology. The near-miss distractors swap in plausible words like 'Usable' or 'Responsive', but the syllabus terms are exactly Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust.

questions.q: Under POUR, full keyboard access and visible focus indicators are examples of which principle?
options: Perceivable
Understandable
Robust
Operable
questions.explanation: Operable means users can operate the interface — this covers full keyboard access, visible focus indicators, and giving users enough time. Perceivable (option A) is about perceiving content, like alt text and colour contrast; the easy mix-up is putting keyboard access under Robust, but Robust specifically refers to semantic markup that works with screen readers and other assistive technology.

questions.q: A team has one afternoon to test whether users can find the 'submit assignment' button in a new layout. Which rung of the fidelity ladder should they build?
options: A polished, full-colour branded mockup, so the button looks appealing enough to find
A fully coded interactive prototype, so they can measure exact click times
A low-fidelity wireframe with boxes and labels, showing where everything sits and the flow to submit
A rough sketch only, because finding a button needs no layout detail at all
questions.explanation: You build a wireframe — low fidelity, boxes and labels — because it tests exactly what matters: can users find and reach the submit button, fast and throwaway. Visual styling is irrelevant to 'can they find the button', so spending the afternoon on a polished mockup (option A) is polishing a prototype like production, the trap. Match the fidelity to the question you are asking.

questions.q: A team is prototyping a new student-portal feature and wants to ensure it is accessible. According to the episode, when should accessibility be built in, and why does this matter?
options: After the interactive prototype tests well, because accessibility only applies to finished, shipped products
From the wireframe stage against POUR, because retrofitting is far harder and failing WCAG can breach the Disability Discrimination Act
Only in the mockup, because accessibility is purely a visual concern about colours and fonts
It is optional for school projects, since legal accessibility duties apply only to commercial e-commerce sites
questions.explanation: Accessibility should be designed in from the wireframe stage against POUR, not bolted on later — the episode's trap is ignoring accessibility in mockups, because retrofitting is far harder than designing it in. It is non-negotiable: both an ethical duty and a legal requirement, and failing W-C-A-G can breach the Disability Discrimination Act (a callback to compliance in 24-06). That legal duty is general, which is why the 'optional for school projects' option is wrong.
